title: Infection Control — Transmission Routes, Chain of Infection, and Precautions
description: NCLEX-style formative assessment covering the chain of infection, transmission-based precautions, and specific infectious agents. Based on Silvestri Practice Quiz content: Foundations of Care → Infection Control (66 questions).

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client with active pulmonary tuberculosis (TB). Which transmission-based precaution type is required in addition to standard precautions?
options: Contact precautions
Droplet precautions
Airborne precautions
Enteric precautions
questions.answer: Airborne precautions
questions.explanation: Tuberculosis is transmitted by airborne droplet nuclei (≤5 µm) that remain suspended in air and can travel long distances. Airborne precautions require a negative-pressure airborne infection isolation room (AIIR) and an N95 respirator for healthcare workers. Standard surgical masks are insufficient.

questions.text: A client has been admitted with bacterial meningitis caused by Neisseria meningitidis. What precaution type should the nurse implement immediately?
options: Airborne precautions
Droplet precautions
Contact precautions
Standard precautions only
questions.answer: Droplet precautions
questions.explanation: Neisseria meningitidis is transmitted via large respiratory droplets (>5 µm) expelled during coughing, sneezing, or close contact. Droplet precautions are maintained for the first 24 hours of effective antibiotic therapy. A surgical mask (not an N95) is worn when within 3–6 feet of the patient.

questions.text: A client presents with cutaneous anthrax. Which transmission route is responsible for this form of anthrax?
options: Airborne — inhalation of spores
Fecal-oral — ingestion of contaminated meat
Contact — direct contact with spores through broken skin
Droplet — respiratory secretions from an infected person
questions.answer: Contact — direct contact with spores through broken skin
questions.explanation: Cutaneous anthrax results from contact transmission — Bacillus anthracis spores enter through a cut or abrasion in the skin, producing a characteristic painless black eschar. Inhalation anthrax results from inhaling spores; gastrointestinal anthrax results from ingesting undercooked contaminated meat. Anthrax is not spread person-to-person through droplets.

questions.text: The nurse is reviewing the six links of the chain of infection. Which link is targeted when a nurse performs hand hygiene before and after patient care?
options: Infectious agent
Reservoir
Portal of exit
Mode of transmission
questions.answer: Mode of transmission
questions.explanation: Hand hygiene breaks the mode of transmission link by removing transient microorganisms from the hands that could be transferred from one surface or patient to another. Hand hygiene is the single most effective infection control measure and interrupts both contact and indirect transmission.

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a neutropenic client with an absolute neutrophil count (ANC) of 400 cells/mm³. Which of the following actions should the nurse implement? Select all that apply.
options: Place the client in a private room with positive-pressure airflow
Restrict fresh flowers and plants in the client's room
Serve the client fresh salads and raw fruits for nutrition
Instruct all visitors to perform hand hygiene before entering
Take the client's temperature rectally for accuracy
questions.answer: Place the client in a private room with positive-pressure airflow
questions.explanation: An ANC of 400 cells/mm³ is severely neutropenic (normal WBC 5,000–10,000/mm³; ANC < 500 requires neutropenic precautions). Correct interventions include: private room with HEPA-filtered positive-pressure air, restricting fresh flowers/plants (source of Pseudomonas and fungi), and strict hand hygiene for all who enter. A low-microbial diet excludes raw fruits and vegetables. Rectal temperatures, suppositories, and enemas are contraindicated in neutropenic clients due to risk of mucosal injury and bacteremia.

questions.text: A client with active TB has been on isoniazid (INH) therapy for three weeks. The client asks when they can return to work. Which response by the nurse is most accurate?
options: After one negative sputum AFB smear is obtained
After two to three continuous weeks on effective therapy and three consecutive negative sputum AFB smears
Only after completing the full 6-month course of therapy
After fever and cough resolve, regardless of sputum culture results
questions.answer: After two to three continuous weeks on effective therapy and three consecutive negative sputum AFB smears
questions.explanation: A client with TB is typically considered non-contagious (and may be released from isolation) after 2–3 continuous weeks of effective therapy AND when three consecutive sputum AFB smears are negative. Both criteria must be met. The full course of TB therapy (6–12 months) continues after the patient is no longer infectious.

questions.text: A family member of a newly diagnosed TB client asks whether they need any treatment. Which of the following prophylactic therapy regimen should the nurse describe?
options: Rifampin for 3 months
Isoniazid (INH) for 6 to 12 months
Ethambutol for 2 months
No treatment is necessary unless the family member develops symptoms
questions.answer: Isoniazid (INH) for 6 to 12 months
questions.explanation: Close contacts (household/family members) of a client with newly diagnosed active TB are placed on prophylactic isoniazid (INH) therapy for 6 to 12 months to prevent progression from latent TB infection to active disease. This is a high-yield NCLEX fact.

questions.text: A client with bacterial conjunctivitis asks what they can do to prevent spreading the infection to family members. Which instruction is most important?
options: Wear a surgical mask when in the same room as family members
Perform frequent hand washing and avoid sharing towels or washcloths
Place the client in a private room with negative-pressure airflow
Limit close contact to only vaccinated family members
questions.answer: Perform frequent hand washing and avoid sharing towels or washcloths
questions.explanation: Bacterial conjunctivitis is transmitted by contact with infected eye secretions, either directly or via contaminated fomites such as towels, washcloths, and pillowcases. Frequent hand washing (before and after touching the eye) and not sharing personal items are the most important preventive measures. A mask is not indicated as conjunctivitis is not a respiratory illness.

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct portal of entry for Mycobacterium tuberculosis?
options: Broken skin or mucous membranes
Respiratory tract — droplet nuclei reach the alveoli
Gastrointestinal tract via ingestion of contaminated water
Bloodstream via percutaneous (needlestick) exposure
questions.answer: Respiratory tract — droplet nuclei reach the alveoli
questions.explanation: TB is an airborne disease. Droplet nuclei (≤5 µm) are inhaled and travel deep into the lung, reaching the alveoli where infection is established. This is the only route of entry for M. tuberculosis in pulmonary TB.

questions.text: A client who had hepatitis A is asking when they can return to their job as a food service worker. The nurse knows the client is no longer infectious after which point?
options: Two weeks after symptom onset
One week after the onset of jaundice
After three consecutive negative stool cultures
When liver function tests return to normal
questions.answer: One week after the onset of jaundice
questions.explanation: A client with hepatitis A is no longer considered infectious one week after the onset of jaundice. Viral shedding in feces is highest 2 weeks before and 1 week after jaundice appears. After 1 week of jaundice, the client is safe to return to food service work from an infection-control standpoint.

questions.text: A nurse is preparing to care for a client on contact precautions. In which order should the nurse don personal protective equipment (PPE)?
options: Mask → gown → gloves → eye protection
Gown → mask → eye protection → gloves
Gloves → gown → mask → eye protection
Eye protection → mask → gloves → gown
questions.answer: Gown → mask → eye protection → gloves
questions.explanation: The CDC-recommended order for donning PPE is: (1) Gown, (2) Mask or respirator, (3) Eye protection (goggles or face shield), (4) Gloves. Gloves are donned last so that their outer surface remains uncontaminated. Doffing order is the reverse, with gloves removed first and hand hygiene performed at each step.

questions.text: A client with a history of chemotherapy presents with a WBC of 2,800 cells/mm³. The nurse recognizes this as which condition?
options: Normal WBC — no action required
Leukocytosis — indicating systemic infection
Leukopenia — requiring infection prevention measures
Agranulocytosis — requiring bone marrow biopsy immediately
questions.answer: Leukopenia — requiring infection prevention measures
questions.explanation: A WBC count of 2,800 cells/mm³ is below the normal range of 5,000–10,000 cells/mm³, indicating leukopenia. Chemotherapy is a common cause of bone marrow suppression and leukopenia. The nurse should implement infection prevention measures, monitor for fever (often the only sign of infection in a leukopenic client), and review orders for neutropenic precautions based on the ANC.

questions.text: A client presents to the emergency department with a sudden high fever of 102.4°F, hypotension, diffuse sunburn-like rash, vomiting, and severe diarrhea. The client reports using a tampon. The nurse suspects toxic shock syndrome (TSS). Which finding, if reported by the client, would cause the nurse to question this diagnosis?
options: Fever greater than 101°F
Sudden onset of hypotension
Vaginal bleeding and discharge
Vomiting and severe diarrhea
questions.answer: Vaginal bleeding and discharge
questions.explanation: Vaginal bleeding or discharge is NOT a sign or symptom of toxic shock syndrome. The classic presentation includes: high fever (≥38.9°C/102°F), sudden hypotension, diffuse macular erythroderma (sunburn-like rash with subsequent desquamation of palms and soles), and multisystem involvement (GI: vomiting/diarrhea; renal, hepatic, muscular). Vaginal bleeding is a distractor commonly included in NCLEX questions on TSS.

questions.text: A nurse assesses a client with a painless, indurated, clean-based ulcer on the genitalia. The nurse knows this type of lesion, called a chancre, is characteristic of which sexually transmitted infection?
options: Genital herpes (HSV-2)
Chancroid (Haemophilus ducreyi)
Syphilis (Treponema pallidum)
Lymphogranuloma venereum (Chlamydia trachomatis)
questions.answer: Syphilis (Treponema pallidum)
questions.explanation: A chancre is the hallmark lesion of primary syphilis. It is characteristically painless and indurated (firm), with a clean base, appearing at the site of inoculation with Treponema pallidum. This is a key NCLEX distinguishing feature — genital herpes lesions are painful and vesicular; chancroid lesions are painful and soft. The chancre typically resolves spontaneously within 3–6 weeks even without treatment, but the infection progresses.

questions.text: A nurse is caring for a client with pelvic inflammatory disease (PID). Which nursing intervention is most appropriate to prevent the spread of infection to the upper abdominal cavity?
options: Place the client in a flat supine position to reduce pain
Position the client in semi-Fowler's position
Encourage frequent douching to cleanse the vaginal canal
Apply a heating pad to the lower abdomen
questions.answer: Position the client in semi-Fowler's position
questions.explanation: Semi-Fowler's position (head of bed elevated 30–45°) uses gravity to promote downward drainage of purulent material in the pelvic cavity, preventing it from tracking upward toward the diaphragm. Abscesses that form high in the abdomen can rupture and cause peritonitis. Frequent douching is contraindicated in PID as it disrupts the natural vaginal flora and can introduce pathogens into the upper reproductive tract.

questions.text: A child is diagnosed with pediculosis capitis (head lice). Which environmental measure should the nurse instruct the parents to perform at home?
options: Steam-clean all carpets and upholstered furniture
Vacuum all floors and upholstered furniture to remove fomites
Spray the home with a commercial pesticide spray
Seal all clothing and bedding in plastic for 24 hours
questions.answer: Vacuum all floors and upholstered furniture to remove fomites
questions.explanation: Vacuuming floors and upholstered furniture removes fomites (lice and nits that have fallen from the hair) from the environment. This is the recommended environmental measure. Bedding and clothing should be washed in hot water (≥130°F) and dried on high heat, or sealed in a plastic bag for 2 weeks. Commercial pesticide sprays are not recommended for home use.

questions.text: A client is admitted with respiratory syncytial virus (RSV). The nurse knows that ribavirin is prescribed for which purpose?
options: Prevention of RSV in high-risk infants (immunoprophylaxis)
Treatment of severe RSV infection in high-risk pediatric clients
Standard first-line treatment for RSV in all age groups
Post-exposure prophylaxis following needlestick with RSV-contaminated blood
questions.answer: Treatment of severe RSV infection in high-risk pediatric clients
questions.explanation: Ribavirin (inhaled) is an antiviral used in the treatment of severe RSV disease in high-risk pediatric clients (e.g., those with congenital heart disease, chronic lung disease, or severe immunocompromise). It is not used for routine RSV treatment in otherwise healthy children. Palivizumab (Synagis) is the agent used for monthly RSV immunoprophylaxis in high-risk infants during RSV season.

questions.text: A client with HIV asks the nurse about their CD4 T-cell count result of 185 cells/mm³. The nurse knows that a CD4 count below which threshold indicates the client has progressed to AIDS?
options: 350 cells/mm³
500 cells/mm³
200 cells/mm³
100 cells/mm³
questions.answer: 200 cells/mm³
questions.explanation: AIDS is diagnosed when an HIV-positive client has a CD4+ T-cell count below 200 cells/mm³ (or when an AIDS-defining illness is present, regardless of CD4 count). A CD4 count of 185 cells/mm³ meets the CDC case definition for AIDS. At this level, the client is at high risk for opportunistic infections such as Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP), toxoplasmosis, and CMV.

questions.text: A nurse is educating a client about the difference between standard precautions and transmission-based precautions. Which statement by the client indicates correct understanding?
options: Standard precautions are only used for clients with a known infection
Transmission-based precautions replace standard precautions for infected clients
Standard precautions apply to all clients; transmission-based precautions are added for specific pathogens
Transmission-based precautions are used only in the intensive care unit
questions.answer: Standard precautions apply to all clients; transmission-based precautions are added for specific pathogens
questions.explanation: Standard precautions are applied to the care of ALL patients, regardless of diagnosis or infection status, based on the principle that any blood, body fluid, or mucous membrane contact may be infectious. Transmission-based precautions (contact, droplet, airborne) are ADDED ON TOP of standard precautions for clients known or suspected to carry pathogens requiring additional control measures — they do not replace standard precautions.

questions.text: A noncompliant client with active tuberculosis refuses to take their medications at home. Which intervention is most appropriate to ensure the client receives therapy?
options: Hospitalize the client involuntarily for the full course of treatment
Arrange for directly observed therapy (DOT) through the local health department
Discontinue medication and reassess in one month
Educate the client again about the importance of compliance and reschedule follow-up
questions.answer: Arrange for directly observed therapy (DOT) through the local health department
questions.explanation: Directly observed therapy (DOT) is the standard intervention for noncompliant TB clients. In DOT, a healthcare worker directly observes the client swallowing each dose of medication. DOT is arranged through the local public health department. It ensures medication adherence, prevents the development of multidrug-resistant TB (MDR-TB), and protects the community from ongoing transmission.

questions.text: A client asks the nurse how the body's normal vaginal flora protects against infection. The nurse's best response addresses which link in the chain of infection?
options: Infectious agent — normal flora competes with pathogens
Mode of transmission — normal flora prevents transmission
Portal of entry — intact mucous membranes and normal flora prevent colonization
Susceptible host — normal flora reduces the host's susceptibility
questions.answer: Susceptible host — normal flora reduces the host's susceptibility
questions.explanation: Normal vaginal flora (predominantly Lactobacillus species) produces lactic acid, maintaining an acidic pH that is inhospitable to pathogens. This mechanism reduces the host's susceptibility to infection — acting on the 'susceptible host' link of the chain of infection. Disruption of normal flora (e.g., through frequent douching, antibiotic use) increases susceptibility to conditions such as bacterial vaginosis and PID.

questions.text: True or False: An airborne infection isolation room (AIIR) used for tuberculosis patients uses positive-pressure airflow to protect the patient from environmental pathogens.
options: True
False
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. An AIIR used for TB (and other airborne pathogens) uses NEGATIVE-pressure airflow — air flows INTO the room from the hallway and is exhausted to the outside or through HEPA filtration, preventing airborne pathogens from escaping into the hallway. POSITIVE-pressure rooms (protective environment) are used for severely immunocompromised clients such as bone marrow transplant recipients — they protect the patient from environmental organisms. Confusing these two is a common NCLEX error.

questions.text: Describe the key distinguishing feature that differentiates airborne precautions from droplet precautions, including the type of respirator required for each.
answer: Airborne precautions are for pathogens carried on droplet nuclei (≤5 µm) that remain suspended in air; require N95 respirator and negative-pressure room. Droplet precautions are for large droplets (>5 µm) that travel <3–6 feet; require standard surgical mask.
Airborne: droplet nuclei, N95, negative-pressure AIIR. Droplet: large droplets, surgical mask, spatial separation.

Droplet precautions use a surgical mask for particles >5 µm traveling less than 6 feet. Airborne precautions require an N95 respirator and negative-pressure room for particles ≤5 µm that remain suspended in air.
questions.explanation: The particle size and behavior distinguish these precaution types. Droplet nuclei (≤5 µm, airborne precautions) remain suspended in air currents and can travel long distances — examples include TB, varicella, measles. Large droplets (>5 µm, droplet precautions) fall rapidly within 3–6 feet and do not remain airborne — examples include influenza, meningococcal meningitis, pertussis. The N95 respirator filters at least 95% of airborne particles; a standard surgical mask does not provide this level of protection and is inadequate for airborne pathogens.
